Metrics Query Path

The block-pruning and aggregation pipeline for a TraceQL metrics query. The same pruning stages as a filter query narrow the candidate blocks, then matching spans are fed into a metrics aggregation engine.

Open Interactive Demo
Step through the metrics aggregation pipeline with block pruning
→

How It Works

A metrics query follows the same pruning pipeline as a filter query, but instead of returning individual spans, the matching spans are fed into an aggregation engine that produces time series. The pruning stages eliminate blocks that cannot contain matching data, then each surviving block is read and its matching spans contribute to the aggregation result.

Metrics Pipeline

TraceQL supports several metrics aggregation functions that operate on the filtered span set:

  • rate() — spans per second over the query window
  • count_over_time() — total count of matching spans per interval
  • histogram() — distribution of an attribute across matching spans
  • quantile_over_time() — percentile values (p50, p95, p99) over time

Each function receives every span that passes the filter predicate and produces one or more time series as output.

Grouping

The by() clause controls how results are split into separate time series. For example, by(resource.service.name) produces one series per service. The grouping key columns must be loaded from each matching span — they are added to wantColumns during predicate building so that parseBlockColumnsReuse decodes them alongside the filter columns.

vs Filter Query

Metrics queries typically scan wider time ranges than filter queries (e.g., “last 1 hour” vs “last 5 minutes”). This means fewer blocks are pruned by the time-range stage, and more blocks survive to the I/O stage. The tradeoff is intentional: accurate aggregation requires comprehensive coverage of the query window.

Optimization

When by() clauses reference intrinsic columns (such as resource.service.name or duration), those values can be resolved from the intrinsic section of the block without decoding the full column data. This avoids extra column decoding overhead for the most common grouping keys.

Pruning in Practice

StageBlocks remainingReduction
Start100—
Time range5050% outside window
KLL range index2060% have max status_code < 500
Block bloom1525% lack status_code ≥ 500
Block I/O + Aggregation1585% eliminated before any read

Key Invariant

One I/O per block — always read the entire block. Column filtering and metrics aggregation are always in-memory.
